data.title: Too Many AI Winners To Settle For Super Micro Computer
data.description:
What matters now is not whether Super Micro Computer (SMCI) can deliver at scale, but whether it is a messy winner or a structurally impaired business due to governance risk. The March 2026 smuggling case with alleged $2.5 billion in smuggled AI hardware and the arrest of co-founder Wally Liaw changes the lens by which investors think about Super Micro. Regardless of the ultimate legal outcome, the pattern here is not an isolated incident, it’s a systemic problem that’s appearing at a time when the infrastructure for AI is becoming more strategically and tightly regulated.

The Cracks Beneath the Surge

The uncomfortable fact is that Super Micro’s business was a harder story to believe in even before the legal news broke. The company reported $12.7 billion in revenue for fiscal Q2 2026, a gain of 123% year-over-year and raised its full-year sales guidance to a minimum of $40 billion.

On the surface, that’s the kind of business that investors love to invest in precisely because they think it’s too good to pass up. The problem is that the quality of the business has changed for the worse. Gross margin came in at 6.3% down from 11.8% a year ago. Cash flow from operations was negative $24 million and free cash flow was negative $45 million. The company’s inventory also climbed to $10.6 billion from $5.7 billion the prior quarter.

I am having difficulty understanding this profile and relating it to the premium story that was historically tied to this name. A company can certainly accept lower margins in a scale phase if they are achieving this scale in a manner that is resulting in sustainable customer lock-in, better working capital, or a rising software and service mix. Super Micro is attempting to prove this out with their DCBBS product, which they say accounted for 4% of their profit in their first half and had margins above 20%.

What I think is more interesting to focus on, however, is the fact that this is a relatively small offset to their core hardware business. Their core business is still very much levered to low margin, high speed server deployments and this makes them much more vulnerable to pricing and customer concentration issues than their top line alone might suggest.

When Dependence Meets Doubt

Customer concentration was a concern to begin with and it still is but I think it is a bigger concern to me following the recent legal developments than it was before them. Specifically, in their Q2, one of their larger data center customers accounted for about 63% of their overall revenue and 84% of their revenue came from their OEM appliance and large data center segment.

This was a precarious position to begin with and this precarious position was really centered around margin and execution issues. It is an even more precarious position now that their counterparties may reassess their partnership exposure in the middle of an export-sensitive situation.

This is where I think the Nvidia (NVDA) relationship becomes less comfortable than bulls think. Nvidia reaffirmed their commitment to export compliance and does not support systems that are diverted for illegal purposes. This is the right stance to take. It also tells you what you need to know. Nvidia doesn’t have an incentive to lean in harder on a partner that’s suddenly becoming a reputational and regulatory risk.

On the other hand, Dell (DELL) is not standing still. They announced quarterly AI optimized server revenue of $9 billion, up 342% year-over-year. They also announced a $43 billion AI backlog at the end of the quarter. They are also making it clear that they are the premier partner for enterprise AI infrastructure built on the Nvidia platform.

I am not saying that Nvidia walks away from Super Micro. I am saying that the burden of proof now lies with Nvidia. I would rather not be the weaker partner in that relationship. The market continues to debate the damage, but the damage is already done at a strategic level. The formal worst case may be obvious, but the real damage may be happening in ways that are harder to see and harder to quantify.

Cheap on Paper But Expensive in Assumptions

Many might fall for the valuation but that’s exactly where investors can get misled. At these price levels, you are not only assuming that the revenue base is legitimate but also that it is sustainable without significant impact from compliance remediation, customer attrition, or partner repricing. That is a lot to assume at a cheap multiple.

The reality is that the warning signs were out there all along. Margins were eroding, cash flow was weak and the customer concentration was extremely high, even before the scandal broke. The legal issue did not create the problem, it exposed underlying weaknesses that were already present. The reason the stock is cheap is not because people are ignoring a good business, but because people are discounting the uncertainty of how sustainable the revenue is and at what margins.

The reason I wouldn’t invest, even at a low multiple, is that I would like to see significant improvement in gross margins, a less concentrated customer base and significant progress on the compliance front before I believe the earnings power behind the current valuation. What would change my view of the bear case?

The actual risk to a negative view, in my opinion, isn’t that the stock goes up. It likely can. Very volatile infrastructure companies with poor fundamental narratives often have vicious rallies. The actual risk to a negative view would be that I incorrectly label a significant but transitory problem in corporate governance as a fundamental problem in the business.

Relief Rally or Real Recovery? The Burden of Proof Is Rising

If customers continue to order, Nvidia support remains functional and DCBBS really does start to lift mix and profitability over the next two quarters, then perhaps the current drawdown in the stock has been a panic selling event rather than the start of a fundamental de-rating. There are also legal factors to consider. Super Micro has claimed that it is not a defendant in this case and has cooperated with the authorities. In this case, perhaps some of the worst fears that investors have about this stock might not be realized.

However, unless gross margins start to expand significantly from their current levels, unless the 63% customer concentration issue somehow resolves itself in the future and unless revenues start to grow much more rapidly than they have in this latest report, then perhaps this would be confirmation that the business was indeed weaker than the revenues indicated even before this scandal began.

Bottom Line

I wouldn’t touch this stock. It’s a speculative play with too many unknowns and I don’t like the risk. I don’t see the need to speculate in a market that’s filled with good quality AI plays.

data.title: Salesforce: Market Is Pricing It At Doomsday Valuations
data.description:
Salesforce: Wipeout is not imminent

Back in my previous update on Salesforce (CRM), I mentioned that the company is going nowhere, that they are here to stay for the long term, and CRM's recent fourth quarter earnings highlights validate my conviction. Yet, after a short respite in early March, even as the Iranian conflict intensified, CRM stock is yet again tethering dangerously closer to those downcast February moments once more. I thought the market would have banished the ghosts of the so-called “2028 Global Intelligence Crisis” for good, but it seems like the worries haven't been fully extinguished. However, the market is having other ideas right now. They are scrutinizing software companies that have overexerted themselves in using stock based compensation, that Wall Street is now asking more questions about, as investors wonder whether their earnings quality could still hold up in a world where "AI eats software," and cause the long duration terminal value computations to be lowered significantly.

In case, you have not realized, the application software industry have gone through one of the worst hammerings in the last decade, and I think that characterization is not even close. Despite analysts upgrading their forward earnings estimates for the industry, the multiples continue to go lower through February this year, as investors digested the multitudinous bearish takes on the long-term durability and sustainability of SaaS as we know it.

Questions are now being asked whether these software companies will still be relevant in an era where we can simply vibe code, where Anthropic’s AI coding tools (ANTHRO) become even more intelligent, and capable, and, more worryingly, highly effective AI agents build software replacements for solutions provided by application SaaS companies.

It is no longer just about overlaying over the SaaS interface, but customizing with a new interface by the development teams within the company themselves. For AI pure plays, they probably see this potential market share grab as the first step in entrenching themselves into the broader enterprise workloads.

However, it is also notable that the software TAM is possibly nowhere near enough to justify the increasingly close to one trillion dollar valuation they see of OpenAI (OPENAI) right now. Ultimately, for these highly valued AI companies to survive and even justify their valuations, killing off software companies per se will not be enough, I think. They have to find a way through to disrupt the labor market in a big way to accrue value, as the labor factor or cost is arguably one of the most significant and appealing TAM for AI pure plays to go for and prove to the market that they can command increasingly higher valuations into the multi trillion dollar mark through the rest of the decade.

In a recent podcast over the weekend, Nobel Prize winning economist and New York Times bestselling author Daron Acemoglu shared helpful insights to let us understand some of the most pertinent and crucial questions about the unfolding (miracle or carnage, depending on which side you are on) artificial intelligence revolution and what it means for the economy, our society, and our precious democracy. And if you think that you are expecting something diplomatic to come out from the Nobel Prize winner, you will be surprised, as he informed us about what the AI companies could be thinking:

There is so little that these companies are doing in order to understand what work humans do and try to be useful to humans. The whole agenda of all of the leading companies in the United States and now joined by DeepSeek in China is AGI, artificial general intelligence. That is a banner for saying these models are going to do everything better than humans. Which of course then leads to the corollary that a lot of companies should just throw away their humans and use these companies. That is an automation agenda. - Bloomberg podcast on “AI Is Being Built to Replace You—Not Help You”

Salesforce is built for the AI agentic era

The way I see it right now, I don't see that companies are forsaking spending on the leading software companies like Salesforce imminently. CRM owns its space. The company is still estimated to deliver $46.1 billion in revenue for FY2027, and CRM’s long-term outlook suggests that the road to $63 billion is possible, despite all the disruption fears going on right now.

Although the earnings revisions for Salesforce are mixed, they are not downright gloomy, and I think that's what you need to know. As long as the outlook is mixed, it should give us some confidence (at the very least) that unless we are looking at a true apocalypse, all the software stocks, including the best ones with tremendous moats, should not face immediate or even medium-term wipeout. We should try not to be too pessimistic at this moment and think that every company out there is going to throw away/discard Salesforce from their suite of enterprise software and go into vibe coding it themselves.

Furthermore, the $800 million for Agentforce AI ARR gives me confidence that the company is making some progress. Could that acceleration be faster? Of course, why not? Salesforce definitely has the potential to do a lot better. We all do. But the market appears to be looking for constant validation after validation and is not willing to put faith into Salesforce long term numbers.

After all, the company has even derived a new (buzzword) called agentic work unit, or AWU. Through this new development, Salesforce wants us to know that companies are deploying AI agents through its platform already, and accelerating.

Remember, for AI tools to work, they require context, they require data, and they require an adaptive system that helps companies navigate which AI tools for which solution. So, Salesforce has all of this orchestrated on its ecosystem.

Can any company simply just override almost decades of data and learning from vibe coding? I'm really not so sure if they can, even if they would like to. They are intelligent tools, but they are not almighty, right? They still need to plug in to Salesforce, and you can bet that Salesforce is not going to let these companies pipe out their data for free, absolutely no way.

They will have to pay the toll, right? There goes my next point. Well, the seat base pricing model could come under more pressure, as enterprise companies use the new agentic coding opportunities to review their contracts, or to delay upgrading to more expensive or premium solutions offered by Salesforce.

But, the company also has the potential to monetize the tokens, or in this case the AWUs, over time. In Salesforce parlance, AWU represents the work done by the AI agents, offering additional and more helpful insights as compared to just tokens, which primarily represent spending.

Therefore, I believe it gives investors a more helpful and broader overview about Salesforce's ability to price consumption-based models accordingly, based on the value that they offer to the enterprise customers. We shouldn't assume that Salesforce will be short-changed just because they face tremendous pressure, as we read, or when companies appear to want to reduce their spending, even though that hasn't really happened because I don't see any assessments for Salesforce being written down in unison across Wall Street, as I enunciated earlier.

Buy Or Sell CRM Stock?

With that in mind, I find it really incredulous that CRM is trading at the valuation that makes it look absolutely pathetic, but also not justified in my view. At just under 14x forward earnings, has the market gone bonkers, valuing one of the most profitable software companies with such a solid moat at well under the S&P 500’s 20x metric?

It’s also significantly below the SaaS average of 22.7, representing not just a simple disconnect, but I believe an unlikely doomsday scenario that the thought experiment relating to what the "2028 Global Intelligence Crisis" wants us to believe.

This is the moment where we must take courage, dive in, and be willing to embrace opportunity when all the rest just see risks. It wouldn't be an easy ride, and it wouldn't be a smooth one either. But hang on tight, be willing to wait patiently, and the mean reversion will pan out over time, I believe.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Burke & Herbert Post-Merger In 2026 - Not My First Pick
data.description:
I've been looking more into regional US banks as of late - in search of value without too much correlation to other factors. While some regional banking institutions have too much CRE exposure or too many factors that make them risky by their own right, there are some gems that I've uncovered worthy of investing in at the right price. And some that are worth investing in right now. Today, I'll look at the result of a merger which I covered about 2 years ago and more - Burke & Herbert (BHRB). My last article on the company can be found here.

While the results of the merger (at the time, the company seemed to be about to grow more) have been going well, the bank has also been going up and down quite a it. For the past year, the total RoR is less than 5.5%, and for the 10-year period, BHRB has managed an RoR of only 26.6%.

This is generally less than the overall financial sector, and it's certainly a lot less than my own personal RoR. This implies to me that BHRB is quite volatile, and likely (barring changes), to continue to be somewhat volatile.

However, there's a set of estimates currently relevant that put the company at an upside for the next 2-3 years. It's my intention with this article to clarify this potential upside, to determine its likelihood, and to work out what the company could be worth as of March 2026. I will note, however, that the company, compared to other regional banks that I cover, tends to be smaller (in terms of market capitalization). A smaller size can be both positive and negative. I will speak to both sides of this equation here.

Let's start with the positives.

Burke & Herbert - Upside requires an outperformance, not just valuation recovery

From the get-go, and from my last article, I called this company a "speculative" investment. In this context, it means that I did not invest in the business. Burke & Herbert is now a regional banking play with a market capitalization just shy of $950M. This makes it relatively small. The company does not come with an S&P Global credit rating, and it comes with a yield of 3.56% at a current P/E multiple of 8x. This makes it lower valued than most regional peers, but it also makes the yield at this multiple lower than its regional and national (as well as international) peers.

If you were "with me" on my last coverage of regional banks, Huntington (HBAN), you will have noted that I spend a lot of energy speaking to asset quality, NPL's, NPA's, and the like to determine the quality of a bank or financial institution that I look at. Unfortunately, this is also where BHRB shows its different colors to some of its peers - and this is unfortunate, because this can have a lot of reasons (and few of them are good).

BRHB, as of the latest results, has shown clear indicators of deterioration in loan and asset quality. NPL's are up to 1.6% in 3Q25 (down again in 4Q), which is more than 2.5x that of HBAN. Compare it to a major bank in Scandinavia, and it's 50-100x higher - but given that we're looking at regionals, we'll keep things regional and US-based. This increase in "bad loans" was from a 0.64% about 2 years ago.

This rise brings to light a few other risks, because changes here are interconnected. The company's reserve coverage is dropping. Allowances for credit losses (as % of NPL's) are now down to less than 76%. This means that these allowances don't necessarily cover the NPLs, which implies that the bank may be (not certain, it may, depending on development) underreserved for defaults.

Unfortunately, unlike HBAN, BHRB has a high exposure to CRE. This is the thing I liked HBAN for not having, and it's therefore, on the same logic, something I will mark BHRB down for. The unfortunate part is that, which I also pointed out in my latest article, the merger with Summit has actually increased the commercial real estate exposure of the bank. HBAN has less than 15%. BRHB has more than 55% of its loans in CRE, which comes to over 310% of RBC (risk-based capital).

What exactly does this concentration mean to you as an investor?

It means that if you want to invest in BHRB, you should be very knowledgeable about the CRE sector, specifically in the bank's focal areas - otherwise, you may be swimming in very deep waters indeed.

This sort of exposure means that over half of the banks' loans would suffer if the CRE sector was to be revaluated, or if interest rates were to go up. All of these things would increase the refinancing risk. And the thing is, with the way things seem to be going right now, I do not believe it to be illogical to expect either a revaluation of CRE in the company's focal areas, or, for that matter, an interest in interest rates eventually.

Now, those bullish on BHRB and its exposures would refer to the DMV focus (DC, Maryland, VA), and that property values here generally have been stable or going up, with Maryland here as an example.

My counterpoint to this argument would be that the deterioration in loan quality and NPL's implies to me that the CRE covering those loans is bad, because the NPL itself reflects a failure to meet payments, which in itself is either a sign of a bad lessee or a poor quality CRE - neither of which is a positive.

I therefore say that BHRB should be considered at the very least a higher-risk bank than others, and the market dictating valuation seems to agree.

Then there's the potential merger of LINKBANCORP (LNKB), which I am not a fan of. I've read up on it for the past few days, and it's not something I consider a positive move.

Why don't I like the prospective merger with LINKBANKCORP?

Because the target in this case is a bank that lost over 35% of its market capitalization over the past 4 years. This is also, given the bank's own size, a big acquisition. If it was done 4 years ago, it would be closer to a 50% to the company's own size - and even today, it's a $350M+ M&A, which comes to a third of the size of BHRB.

What also speaks against it is in this deal, the company is taking on a significant (10% of market cap) amount of goodwill. They are further taking on this amount of risk at a time when their own loan quality is sliding. The combined prospective bank will not have a lower, but higher exposure to CRE, particularly in spaces and subsectors that are already subject to vacancies (such as office). Despite what I would consider an equally poor asset quality, implied also by the loss of 40% of share value in 4 years, BHRB is in my view, also overpaying for the bank. At about 1.5x tangible book, the risk is certainly at least worth mentioning that it's overpaying for underperforming assets - unless BHRB knows something we don't about the assets.

But even if that is true, then the M&A will have an immediate negative effect on the company's KPI's. It will, with very little doubt, increase the cost of deposits and decrease its capital ratios, with a CET-1 dropping at the very least 100 bps, and BHRB's CET-1 pre this merger was actually one of its saving graces, because it stands at almost 13%, which is very good for US regional banking.

The goodwill is especially worrying because it amounts to more than 2.5x the amount of goodwill that the company currently holds on its balance sheet. The reason I don't like excess goodwill in a merger I assume, to be of questionable quality is that the risk of impairment charges is high. Remember, Goodwill is the premium paid above the fair market value of tangibles and intangibles - and this is only relevant if the business performs the expected synergies or upside. I expect the risk is high that it, in fact, does not do this.

I believe BRHB's historical performance is indicative of the potential underperformance of this M&A as well, which is why I would demand a significant discount to be interested in investing here.

Do we have this discount?

Burke & Herbert Valuation - discount is there, but it may not be enough.

We have a discount.

Current valuation for the bank is, as I mentioned, about 8x P/E. This is low for regional banking, which trends towards 9-12x P/E. However, as I believe that I have established above, it's not unfair to be worried about BHRB's near-term performance and to question the background and potential of its current merger.

But there is one thing that may justify an investment here. A massive EPS increase. You see, the current analyst average is a 17% AEPS growth in 2027, another 16% in 2028E.

Why?

To understand that, we go back to the positive side of the merger. The bank expects significant cost savings from this merger, as well as expansion into new markets, such as PA, and other states. BHRB will attempt to use the high scale to leverage efficiencies, and current expectations are for an EPS growth of over 30% in total. This also explains the buy-side rationale behind the merger, and I can respect this. Combine such an upside with the leveraging of digital technology, and we may see something like this.

In the event of this sort of earnings growth, even a low-end 8.5x P/E on a forward basis would imply a share price of well over $85/share for 2028E, which would in turn make a strong case for a "BUY" rating here, easily clearing my hurdle of 15% annualized.

The problem I am having with this way of viewing the thesis is that it relies entirely (because it currently trades at 8x P/E) on the realization of that growth. And because of a number of factors, I do not consider that growth to be as easily accessible or realized as the bank or others may expect.

The combination of risks for NPL's, for lower reserves, for CRE exposure, for margin increases (remember, margins went to 34.2% in 4Q, how likely is it that another surge is coming? I say not highly), historical EPS decline rates, with persistent integration challenges with Summit, and with bank-technical risks like AFS securities losses following interest hikes (which is part of my thesis), creates a situation where I do not just want growth-related upside, I actually want reversal upside as well.

I therefore say that the bank is not cheap enough for me yet, and I add my rating to the "HOLD" currently given by both recent SA as well as Quant.

What PT?

With the reservation that this is still a speculative investment, I do not believe that my previously given P/E premium (post-Summit) of 10-12x P/E is justified here. I'd want my hurdle rate to be a conservative P/E valuation on a forward basis. Working from a 25% discount rate for the 2028E results, which I believe to be fair based on the risks mentioned above, and a lower-end 9.5x P/E fair value for the bank calls for investing below $54/share, which will become my 2026E PT for the company. That means that the bank has to drop at least 10% for this to be attractive. This is due to both my discounting and the comparatively lower yield.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Burke & Herbert is an interesting regional banking play in a mostly attractive area of the United States, but unfortunately, the bank has been seeing challenging trends both from an operational and integration/merger side. Put into context, I can buy BHRB - or I can buy a 4%+ yielding better banks with a BBB+ credit rating, and a significantly better conservatively-adjusted upside available than this one. In such a situation, my choice is obvious. This investment is, contextually speaking, a "HOLD", even though upside based on its current EPS growth forecast is entirely possible. I simply don't like the risk/reward profile here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $54/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: SAP: Fast Approaching 'Buy' Territory, But I Wouldn't Rush It
data.description:
SAP (SAP) share price has lost more than a third of its value over the past year in a rather unusual bear market for a high-growth and high-margin software giant.

As a result, SAP's 3-year performance is now roughly in line with the broader software sector, and the stock is attracting an increasing amount of value-seeking investors in the highly attractive technology sector.

The spike in trading volume in recent months in combination with ongoing selling pressure, however, is a major warning sign that the worst might not be over yet. Ongoing risks within the equity market only make matters worse, especially for highly cyclical stocks within the technology sector.

On the other hand, sell-side analysts have been reluctant in downgrading SAP, and this has now resulted in a very wide gap between the actual price and the consensus target price (see the graph below). This is yet another warning sign for investors, as stock prices tend to bottom-out once all analysts have already downgraded the company and pessimism hits peak levels.

Meanwhile, the earnings multiple of 25 is at much more reasonable levels for a company with industry-leading margins that is expected to grow at low-teen rates.

It is worth mentioning that all this is happening right after I upgraded SAP from "Sell" to a "Hold" in early October of last year. Although I had some reservations when it came to rating SAP as a "Buy," there were some profitability tailwinds that did not justify the "Sell" thesis anymore.

Sector Headwinds

Even after the sharp drop in recent months, SAP does not appear to be undervalued relative to its peers. Price/sales multiples of large software companies still exhibit a relatively strong relationship with net income margins, and in that regard, SAP appears fairly valued.

What that means is that in recent months we observe a relative flattening of the trendline drawn above as multiples within the whole sector decompress. Even the high-margin and high-quality large players in the space have experienced drops of around 25% and above over the past six months.

This supports a thesis that SAP's recent performance is largely due to wider sector-related headwinds as opposed to specific factors related to SAP.

When it comes to these industry-wide headwinds, it's quite difficult to become optimistic about SAP yet, as yields are likely headed higher and we will most likely continue to experience sector rotation into less-cyclical areas of the market.

What About The Business?

When looking at SAP's business, however, it is difficult to justify the recent sell-off. On the surface there are some concerns regarding the company's ongoing AI pivot and its recently introduced AI-tools. While these might be very concerning for narrative-driven investors, they are not of particular interest to long-term investors.

Quarterly margins have been somewhat lower in the past two quarters, but overall they remain near record-high levels. More importantly, based on the recently provided guidance for fiscal year 2026, SAP management expects profitability improvements alongside robust revenue growth.

Cloud revenue is expected to grow at 24% in the mid-range at constant currency. This is slightly lower than the 27% expected a year ago for FY 2025. However, the total revenue growth is expected to accelerate through 2027, and operating expenses are projected to grow at a lower rate, which means higher margins (provided that gross profitability does not deteriorate).

Free cash flow is also expected to grow in 2026, and based on the projection shown above, SAP now trades at a forward free cash flow yield of around 5.6%. On a historical basis, this is very attractive pricing. SAP rarely traded at yields of around 5% over the past decade or so. The last time this happened was in 2020 when SAP's share price fell sharply in the last months of the year.

Investor Takeaway

SAP's recent share price rout is largely as a result of industry- and market-wide dynamics. Despite certain company-specific issues, the company is on track to further expand its margins while continuing to grow its topline at very high rates. The stock is now priced at fair-value given the current economic conditions, which resulted in a notable multiple contraction across the whole software sector. These conditions, however, would most likely continue to deteriorate in the near-term which is the main reason why I leave SAP's rating as a "Hold." Long-term investors with enough dry gunpowder to endure some short-term volatility, however, could pay less attention to macro factors and initiate a position at current levels.

data.title: Lam Research: Strong Growth Ahead For This Expensive Stock
data.description:
By Khaveen Jey, CFA, FMVA, Portfolio Manager @ Khaveen Investments & Nicholas Tan, Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Lam Research (LRCX), we determined that the company had a strong market positioning in the etch market, due to its early mover advantage, which had contributed towards long-standing customer relationships. We further expected the company to sustain a strong presence in the etch market, supported by its developments in its core etch products, such as Akara, in early 2025.

Since our last coverage, its stock price has surpassed our previous price target of $127.53 (22/10/2025) and is currently trading at $244.25 (24/02/2026), which is almost double our previous target price. We also found that its H1 2025 revenue growth (24.87%) was much higher than our 2025F projections (8.45%). In this analysis, we thus analyze why revenue grew much higher than our forecasts, and whether the company could sustain its revenue growth. We first evaluate the reasons that our projections were not in line. We then analyze whether China could sustain its revenue growth, as the region accounts for the largest portion of its revenues and has the 2nd highest geographic growth. We then evaluate whether the transition to Gate-All-Around (GAA) could provide market growth opportunities, as management indicated that the transition to GAA “equates to roughly $1 billion in incremental Lam SAM for every 100,000 wafer starts per month of capacity”.

Higher-Than-Expected Growth

We first analyze the reasons that our previous projections were not in line. We thus compile their revenue breakdown by end market and our previous projections to evaluate this further. We also compile their respective market breakdown to compare their growth against the market.

In H1 2025, the company grew by 24.87% YoY and surpassed our previous revenue forecasts (8.45%). From 2022 to H1 2025, the company’s foundry growth has consistently been higher than the market, which we believe could be due to its wide product portfolio and early mover advantage, allowing the company to build longer-lasting customer relationships, as we highlighted in our previous analysis. The company also exceeded our growth forecasts, mainly due to its high foundry growth, which advanced 97.16% YoY and surpassed our previous foundry growth projections (10.83%). According to management, they highlighted “foundry strength came from investments at the leading edge in addition to mature node spending that we saw in China”.

Foundry

We thus examine this further and analyze whether leading-edge capacity increased foundry growth. We thus compile TSMC and Samsung’s (SSNLF) leading-edge (7nm and below) capacity increase as a proxy for their capex for leading-edge nodes, as TSMC and Samsung account for 32% of 2025 revenue.

We see that its significant customers (TSMC and Samsung) had increased their total 2nm capacity by 518.8%, which is higher than the H1 2025 foundry growth of 97.2% and could explain its high foundry growth. In 2025, their annual capacity also increased by 68.6% YoY, which is lower than H1 2025 foundry growth, but is still quite high and can explain its high foundry growth.

If we compare TSMC and Samsung’s H1 2025 combined geographic growth (Taiwan and Korea) of $728 million against H1 2025 foundry growth of $3,155 million, it is still short $2,427 million, highlighting that there are other unaccounted factors. China grew by $1,240 million, and combined with Taiwan and Korea, their total growth is $1,968 million, which can explain 62% of foundry growth. We thus compile China’s mature nodes and leading-edge nodes' capacity increase to analyze China’s growth.

China’s annual mature node capacity grew by 10.13% in 2025, which is a quarter of China's H1 2025 growth of 42.7%, and is unable to explain the large increase in its H1 2025 foundry growth. To further our analysis in China, we analyze whether it could be company-specific reasons, as China's leading-edge and mature-node capacity growth is unable to explain the large change in H1 2025 foundry growth. We filter out the largest Chinese foundries from the top 10 largest global foundries in our source and compile their capex growth. Based on our analysis, we found that the largest Chinese foundries had decreased their capex by 6.54% for 2025, which contradicts the large increase in their H1 2025 foundry growth. Assuming that the largest Chinese foundries had lowered equipment spending and that the company still benefited, we suspect it could be that Chinese foundries are switching suppliers from their competitors to them. This would be a reasonable assumption, as this would validate both the decline in capex and the higher foundry growth. Based on our analysis, its 3 largest competitors’ China revenue have either declined or are expected to decline. For example, Tokyo Electron (TOELY) Q3 & Q4 CY2025’s China revenue had declined by 18% or $520 million, while Applied Materials Q3 & Q4 CY2025’s China revenue decreased 7.8% or $320 million. While ASM (ASM) (deposition) did not disclose its China revenue, ASM “projected a double-digit year-on-year decrease in 2026 China revenue”. As such, we believe this indicates that Chinese suppliers are switching from their competitors to them, as their competitors’ China revenue declines and their China revenue grows substantially. We also believe this highlights the company’s strong competitiveness and its strength in the foundry segment, as customers are switching from competitors to it.

Memory

In H1 2025, memory growth (-1.3%) was slightly lower than our projections (5.9%) and is also much lower than the memory market growth rate (35.1%). To better analyze its H1 2025 foundry growth, we compile its reported memory customers and their respective memory capex. For Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron (MU), we compile their memory capex from our source, while we estimated Kioxia’s 2025 capex using its Q3 2025 YTD capex growth.

In 2025, Samsung’s capex declined by -10.6% YoY, which represents the largest decline in memory capex. Samsung is also one of the company’s largest customers, accounting for either 15% or 17% of total revenue. Other than that, SK Hynix’s memory capex also decreased by 4.3% for 2025 and could have likely contributed towards lower memory growth. As such, we believe that the slight decline in memory may be due to Samsung or SK Hynix.

Logic / IDM

In H1 2025, Logic/IDM's actual growth (-60.6%) has also underperformed our previous growth projections (8.6%) and is also much lower than the logic market growth (42.0%). Previously, between FY2023 and FY2021, one of the company’s significant customers (> 10% revenue) was Intel, an IDM. Currently, Intel (INTC) is still one of the company’s reported customers. In 2025, Intel’s capex has declined by 38.8%, as the company focused on reducing capital investments amid lower margins. Intel’s large capex decline aligns well with the large logic/IDM decline. As such, we believe that the large decline in IDM growth can likely be attributed to Intel.

We overall determine that one of the company’s key strengths is its foundry segment, where the company has grown faster than the foundry market (2022 to H1 2025). This can also be further evidenced by its large market share in deposition (17%) and etching (39%), whereby etching, deposition, and lithography are the 3 key processes for enabling the fabrication of chips with smaller process nodes and higher density. We thus believe that the reason for its higher-than-expected revenue growth can be attributed to its strong foundry competitiveness, as its H1 2025 foundry segment grew by 97% YoY. Due to its foundry competitiveness, we expect the company to retain its leading position in etching and a strong positioning in deposition. Despite its strong growth, its logic/IDM weighed down its H1 2025 growth, which we suspect is likely due to Intel, as the company is undertaking cost-saving measures and has cancelled manufacturing projects in Germany and Poland to reduce capex. We also believe that the weaker-than-expected growth from memory can be due to Samsung and SK Hynix, as their capex has slowed for 2025, and as Samsung is one of their largest customers (>10% revenue).

Short-term China Weakness

In its Q2 FY2026 earnings transcript, management indicated “China being more kind of flattish year-on-year”, suggesting lower short-term growth from China. We thus analyze whether its long-term outlook is robust and could sustain its growth outlook.

China’s revenue contribution has grown considerably from 18% in 2015 to 39% in H1 2025 and currently accounts for the largest geographic contribution. China also has the 2nd-highest revenue growth among all the segments, highlighting its strong geographical growth. In 2024, China’s growth declined slightly by 1.41%, whereby management indicated that they “lost a bunch of customers in China that largely purchase Reliant systems”. Reliant Systems is the company’s etching, deposition, and cleaning products for CMOS above 14nm. In its Q2 FY2025 earnings transcript, the company explained that its Chinese customers were forecasted to spend $700 million in FY2025, with “a lot of that” going to Reliant. However, they indicated that these customers were subsequently prevented from purchasing their products due to the new semiconductor restrictions implemented. In December 2024, the US introduced new wafer fabrication equipment export restrictions and added more Chinese entities to the entity list, whereby US companies are prohibited from selling or buying goods to those entities without a governmental license. Due to the new US restriction, the company expected a loss of $700 million in China revenue, highlighting the impact of the US restriction on its revenue.

Recently, the company also indicated that the new US restriction is expected to lower China’s growth by $600 million for 2026. The newly introduced 50% affiliate rule restricts some of its Chinese customers from buying its products. The 50% affiliate rule prevents Chinese companies from abusing a loophole that uses unlisted subsidiaries to purchase US semiconductor products. The rule prevents this loophole by restricting entities that have at least 50% or more ownership by a company on the restricted list. Alongside the previous restriction, both restrictions highlight that the company’s growth has been weakened by trade restrictions and could further worsen due to unexpected trade restrictions.

Beginning in 2018, the US began implementing semiconductor export controls against China to maintain the US’s semiconductor technological leadership and prevent China’s technological advancements. This includes bans on EUV lithography machine exports to China, crucial for Chinese foundries to manufacture 7nm and below chips, as well as wafer fabrication equipment restrictions. From 2022 to 2025, more semiconductor restrictions were introduced with higher tariffs. Due to potentially worsening restrictions, it was reported that Chinese foundries began stockpiling more wafer fabrication equipment, leading to “elevated inventories” and that China is currently facing “overcapacity concerns”.

In H1 2025, Japan climbed the largest by 62.13% YoY, with the company indicating “there's a large new foundry in Japan”. To analyze this further, we first compiled the 5 largest foundries by revenue in Japan, but filtered the non-Japanese companies as they do not disclose specific geographic capex.

Based on the table above, we believe that Rapidus is likely the key reason for higher Japan growth, as Rapidus capex growth (55.6%) aligns relatively well with its Japan growth (62.1%). Rapidus was also recently established in 2022 by 8 Japanese companies with JPY7.3 billion, with $36 billion in expected investments across this decade for manufacturing 2nm chips.

For H1 2025, Southeast Asia also grew strongly by 34.59% YoY. Similar to Japan, we compile the 5 largest foundries by revenue in Southeast Asia and filter out 1 company that did not disclose geographic capex.

We believe that it's likely X-Fab (XFABF) that has contributed towards the company’s Southeast Asia H1 2025 growth, as their capex ($650 million) is above the company’s Southeast Asia growth of $184 million, while Silterra ($0.10 billion) and SSMC ($0.06 billion) change in growth is below Southeast Asia’s growth. X-Fab was recently announced in 2025 to improve “wafer production capacity from 30,000 to 40,000 units per month”.

From 2022 to H1 2025, Europe’s revenue declined from $1,220 million to $242 million. To analyze this further, we compile the 5 largest foundries by revenue in Europe and their respective capex. We then remove Nexperia as they are a subsidiary and do not have a specific capex breakdown.

We find that the capex decline in 2024 and 2025 aligns with its European revenue decline in the same period. This highlights that the decline in European revenue can be attributed to the decline in capex spending by European companies.

We overall believe that China’s short-term growth is weak and can negatively impact its short-term growth, due to intensifying trade restrictions and the historical impact of these trade restrictions on its Chinese customers. We further believe that the current overcapacity issues and elevated inventory would weaken its short-term outlook. Management further indicated that the new trade restrictions had cost the company $600 million in potential revenue for 2026, further showing short-term China weakness. Despite this, we expect China’s growth outlook in the medium to long term to be robust, driven by China’s semiconductor market’s long-term growth drivers such as AI, quantum computing, rising adoption of IoT devices, and its “Made In China 2025” policy. This initiative pushes for China’s 70% self-sufficiency in domestic semiconductor manufacturing by lowering taxes for high-tech companies and offering funding for semiconductor companies. For example, China’s Big Fund Phase 3 has $47.5 billion of funding for its semiconductor companies, which would bolster its market semiconductor growth outlook.

GAA Market Opportunity

After determining that China’s growth would unlikely sustain its growth outlook, we thus examine whether the transition from FinFET to gate-all-around (GAA) could provide any market opportunities. Management highlighted that the “transition to gate all around equates to roughly $1 billion in incremental Lam SAM for every 100,000 wafer starts per month of capacity”. Based on its investor presentation, they also indicated they intend to capture 50% of this incremental SAM growth.

Beginning in 2015, node architecture was initially manufactured using FinFET and was the leading architecture design for process nodes at 14 nm, 10 nm, and 7 nm. However, as process nodes became smaller with higher density, moving from 7nm to 3nm and 2nm, the FinFET was unable to handle smaller process nodes, such as 2nm and below. This is because the “variability in the electrical characteristics of FinFET devices starts to explode”. As such, this increases the need for a new architecture, which could handle the smaller nodes. GAA architecture can handle the smaller nodes, with higher chip performance and lower power consumption compared to FinFET, and is crucial for manufacturing chips 2nm and below. As indicated in our first point, CSPs are expanding their data center capex considerably to meet cloud computing demands and improve AI training infrastructure. Importantly, Oracle (ORCL) and Meta(META) have recently agreed to purchase AMD’s latest EPYC Venice data center CPUs (TSMC’s GAA 2nm), which are expected to enter mass production this year. As shown in our previous analysis of Nvidia (NVDA), Nvidia’s Feynman is also expected to be manufactured using TSMC’s GAA 1.6nm for AI computing in 2028F. These trends highlight that as process nodes become smaller and GAA’s adoption increases by large-scale CSP for cloud computing, the key driver towards GAA transition is AI computing and machine learning, as chips with GAA architecture provide higher performance and lower power consumption compared to FinFET. For example, comparing N2 (GAA) vs N3E (FinFET), N2 provides 10-15% higher performance and 25-30% lower power consumption.

We further compile the company’s and Applied Materials’ GAA-related tools to better analyze whether they could take advantage of the market transition. We were unable to collect GAA-related products from competitors such as Tokyo Electron and ASM International, as their websites do not specifically break down their products into GAA.

We see that the company has a suite of selective etch products, such as Argos, Prevos, and Selis, to take advantage of the market transition from FinFet to GAA. Its Argos enables customers to more effectively modify wafer surfaces, while its Prevos utilizes a new chemical technology for better precision. Its Selis product uses “both radical and thermal etch capabilities to enable ultra-high selective etching with uniform top-to-bottom process control without causing damage to the wafer structure”. The company also recently released its Akara, which has DirectDrive, TEMPO, and SNAP for more precise etch control for chips with shrinking dimensions. We also find that the company has fewer products than Applied Materials for GAA. Despite this, we believe that the company is still positioned well to take advantage of the GAA transition, as its product count, while smaller, is still comparable to Applied Materials, which is a large player in the etch and deposition market.

We overall determine that the company can take advantage of the transition to GAA with its products. We also expect the company’s strong competitive advantage, such as its GAA portfolio and early mover advantage, to support its growth outlook. In Q2 FY2026, they had also seen Akara’s install base double over the last year, highlighting its strong competitiveness. The company also recently introduced VECTOR TEOS 3D for advanced packaging of AI and ML chips, which we believe would improve its competitive advantage in the market and bolster its growth outlook.

We thus update our revenue as our projections were considerably off. For 2025F’s Memory and Logic/IDM growth, we used H1 2025 growth. We tapered down H1 2025 growth to calculate Foundry’s 2025F growth. After 2025F, we used each segment’s market CAGR to project its future growth.

Based on our projections, we forecast the company to grow with a 3-year forward growth rate of 21.9%. To forecast its GAA transition market opportunity, we first compile the global GAA wafer capacity per month. We then calculate the market opportunity for the company by multiplying every 100,000 increase in GAA capacity by $1 billion. We further calculate its incremental SAM opportunity by multiplying 50% against its market opportunity, as the company indicated they are planning to target 50% of growth in incremental SAM. We then add its incremental SAM opportunity to our above revenue projections to derive our total revenue projections for the company.

We calculate that the total market opportunity for the company over the next 2 years is $842 million, which would increase its 2026F and 2027F growth by 1.56% and 0.19%, respectively.

Risk: Intense Competition With Applied Materials

We believe one of the key risks that could negatively impact the company is its intense competition with Applied Materials. In February 2026, Applied Materials released new modification systems such as Viva, Sym3 Z Magnum, and Spectral to improve leading-edge chips’ performance at 2nm and below. In October 2025, Applied Materials also introduced Kinex, Xtera, and PROVision, which further enhance their competitiveness. We further expect Applied Materials to continue developing newer products, which would heighten the competition between Applied Materials and the company.

Valuation

Based on our valuation of the company using a DCF model, incorporating our revenue projections at a 5-year forward average growth of around 17.3%, a discount rate of 15.1% reflecting the company’s WACC, and a terminal value based on the Semiconductor Materials & Equipment sub-industry average EV/EBITDA multiple of 22.33x, our model indicates a downside of 20.69%. This is still a higher price target ($166.15) than we previously forecasted ($127.53) as we now expect the company’s revenue to grow with a 10-year forward growth rate of 13.39%, which is slightly higher than our previous forecast of 8.71%. Our terminal EBITDA value of $534 billion is also higher than our previous terminal EBITDA value of $336 billion, attributable to a higher EBITDA, driven by higher revenue growth.

Verdict

We expect the company’s growth outlook to remain robust, due to its strong foundry competitiveness, large product portfolio, and GAA product portfolios. We also believe its early-mover advantage and product developments, such as Akara and VECTOR TEOS 3D, would further support its growth outlook. Despite short-term headwinds from China, we expect China to support its medium-term and long-term growth outlook, driven by growth drivers such as AI, quantum computing, and China’s push towards 70% domestic semiconductor self-sufficiency. We also expect China to remain one of its largest geographic regions in the medium to long-term. Based on our DCF valuation, we thus rate Lam Research as a Sell, given the massive 190% surge in the stock price over the past year.

data.title: Soitec SA : Photonics Will Shine
data.description:
Intro

Soitec (SLOIF) is a market leader in the design and manufacturing of high-end wafers for the semiconductor industry. Its proprietary technology, called Smart Cut, enables cutting thin layers of high-value substrates and merging them with lower-cost wafers, thereby improving production efficiency.

Serving the smartphone industry as its main market, Soitec has suffered a severe contraction in volumes since 2022, exacerbated by foundries stockpiling its wafers, driven by the supply chain crisis that forced producers to manage working capital with just-in-case inventory management.

To cope with the difficult situation, the firm has engaged in a cost-cutting program, resulting in reductions in OPEX and CAPEX. Also, the current CEO, Pierre Barnabé, has been in charge since 2022 and will step down in the coming month, with Laurent Remont to take over. Laurent has deep expertise in the telecom sector, with prior experience at STMicroelectronics (STM) and Infineon (IFNNY). With client inventory channels gradually improving, growing opportunities in the data center, and a reasonable valuation, it may not be too late to buy the stock.

Investment Thesis

The firm has developed a complete portfolio of wafers aiming to optimize the energy consumption of semiconductors with applications from the mobile segment (Radio frequency RF, Filters with the POI), automotive, and small devices power saving applications, and finally the data center segment in the photonics segment.

Over the recent years, group revenues have increased consistently from 2018 to calendar 2022, driven by the expansion of the mobile segment and the emergence of 4G and 4G frequencies, using RF SOI semiconductor substrates. However, the supply chain over-ordered wafers during the global chip crisis, leading to a period of inventory above trend and a prolonged, difficult phase of undershipping for Soitec operations.

The more recent quarterly trends shown below indicate that the S1 FY2026 has experienced a sharp decline in the mobile and industrial segments. On the positive side, management comments suggest positive trends going forward. We will discuss segment trends in the following.

Soitec's largest segment is the smartphone market. As shown below, global units are still below their 2021 peak, and the recent 2025 rebound could be invalidated this year due to memory shortages and their impact on phone affordability.

At the moment, Soitec's revenues are on the decline due to wafer undershipping to address semiconductor foundry inventories that are still elevated. However, the situation is gradually improving and could potentially mark FY26 as a cyclical bottom. As expressed in the recent quarterly call :

« Mobile Communications generated revenue at EUR 90 million in the third quarter, down 36% year-on-year organically. As expected, RF-SOI volumes remain impacted by customer inventory correction, which is improving, but not yet complete. »

Another reason to see the glass half full is that the substrate content per unit area (mm²) should still increase by 2030, with new frequencies (mmWave and UWB) becoming available in the market.

Within connected devices, Soitec cites edge AI opportunities with smart glasses, which require a long battery life. The market leader, Meta-Ray Ban, has recently increased its 2026 production to at least 20 million units. It remains to be seen whether this can create a market opportunity for low-power semiconductor wafers within Soitec's addressable market. At the moment, it seems that the NXP (NXPI) microcontroller used in the glasses is based on 28-nanometer FD-SOI.

Finally, and potentially the most promising segment of Soitec: Photonics in data centers. Its substrates are used by networking companies such as Broadcom or Marvell Technology to build silicon photonics processors. To support higher data speeds and densities in the new generation of data centers, Cloud hyperscalers are increasingly using light for information transport rather than electrons.

In the following picture, we see that Soitec SOI technology is present in the most advanced technology in the market, namely in Broadcom’s co-packaged optics used in its Tomahawk 6. Furthermore, recent quarterly results indicate strong growth in both Marvell's (MRVL) and Broadcom's (AVGO) networking segments, driven by data center applications. As a result, this could bode well for Soitec’s Photonics segment, which is generating more than $100m in revenue. While current management expectations remain anchored at a 25%/year CAGR, recent market trends suggest a +50% industry growth rate in the Photonic segment.

What valuation can we expect?

The main hypotheses taken into account in my DCF model are :

  • A slow and gradual recovery in the mobile and industrial segment, as inventory levels in semiconductor foundries are still too elevated.
  • A strong growth in the Edge & Cloud segments led by solid fundamentals in Photonics and new applications in connected objects.
  • A low level of CAPEX as factories are only used at 60-70%.

Using a 9% WACC and 3% long-term growth, my DCF target price is USD86 per share, indicating more than 40% further upside potential.

Risks and Opportunities

Soitec has disappointed investors for a couple of years. Overly optimistic growth projections combined with an elevated capex cycle have cost the job to the CEO. His successor, Laurent Remont, will assume the role on April 1. Given the strong stock rebound from recent lows and the light, elevated expectations, it is crucial that the new CEO present a comprehensive, realistic strategy.

Also, many market comments on the stock seem to anticipate a material acceleration in the data center Silicon Photonics segment. While strong results from the US company Lumentum (LITE) suggest that the shift from electron data transfer to photonics is progressing rapidly, I believe Soitec's data center needs to grow by more than 30% per year in the medium term to justify the upside potential.

Conclusion

Soitec has endured tough years, with declining segments amid elevated CAPEX for development. However, recent management comments on the mobile segment could indicate an inflection point by calendar 2027, which could lead to a prolonged cyclical rebound as most of the sales decline is related to undershipping to clean inventory levels in semiconductor factories.

On the growth side, the Photonic segment appears promising and could drive the entire business higher. In the background, the new CEO will take power in a context of ongoing cost-cutting, and therefore with less financial risk for the business. He could move fast and strengthen the fast-growing data center segment. My DCF indicates upside potential. I rate the stock with a BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fidelity Select Consumer Discretionary Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Consumer Discretionary Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • We believe that the market is inherently inefficient and often too short-term-focused, which affords opportunities to generate excess returns with fundamental research utilizing a long-term investment horizon.
  • We prefer companies where business quality or forward prospects seem underappreciated by the market and focus on identifying attractive relative value, including considering opportunities created by temporary issues or turnarounds.
  • Stock selection and idea generation come from bottom-up research that leverages Fidelity's deep and experienced global consumer team. We also consider attractive consumer stocks outside of the benchmark that offer the potential for favorable risk-adjusted returns.
  • Position sizing is based on conviction in the fundamental thesis and assessment of risk/reward.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

Fund Information

Manager(s): Jordan Michaels

Trading Symbol: FSCPX

Start Date: June 29, 1990

Size (in millions): $1,018.11

Morningstar Category: Fund Consumer Cyclical

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. The consumer discretionary industries can be significantly affected by the performance of the overall economy, interest rates, competition, consumer confidence and spending, and changes in demographics and consumer tastes. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. The fund may have additional volatility because of its narrow concentration in a specific industry. Non-diversified funds that focus on a relatively small number of stocks tend to be more volatile than diversified funds and the market as a whole.

Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Review

Consumer discretionary stocks returned -0.43% in the last quarter of 2025, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Discretionary 25/50 Index, finishing behind the 2.66% advance of the S&P 500® index amid softening consumer sentiment, persistent inflation, continued uncertainty surrounding trade and tariffs and bifurcated spending. Meanwhile, the broader U.S. stock market was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's two interest-rate reductions.

Looking at the consumer, spending remained stable during Q4, supported by positive real wage growth and strong household balance sheets. However, spending has been increasingly supported by higher-income consumers, with lower cohorts experiencing a deceleration in real wage growth and less exposure to asset appreciation. Additionally, after two years of near-zero goods inflation, tariff hikes triggered a rise in goods prices over the second half of 2025. Inflation remained sticky in housing and services, keeping headline consumer inflation elevated and above the Fed's 2% target.

Among the industries that make up the MSCI sector index, most auto-related industries underperformed this quarter. Automotive retailers (-11%) and automotive parts & equipment (-3%) stocks faced tariff pressures. Auto retailers were particularly hurt by a slowdown in the do-it-yourself business, given high inflation. Home categories, including homebuilding (-10%) and home improvement retail (-12%), also declined. Elevated mortgage rates, a stagnant housing market and increased economic uncertainty continued to weigh on these groups.

Conversely, apparel retail (+13%) and the apparel, accessories & luxury goods (+9%) group each gained in Q4, with many companies in these categories benefiting from solid quarterly financial results and company-specific factors that elevated the stock prices.

Largest Contributors Vs. Benchmark

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund Retail Class shares advanced 1.50%, outperforming the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Discretionary 25/50 Index.

An overweight and stock selection in apparel retail contributed to the fund's performance versus the MSCI sector index in Q4, along with underweights in automotive retail and education services. The fund's top-two largest individual relative contributors were out-of-benchmark positions in Canadian apparel retailer Aritzia (+42%) and Dollar Tree (+30%), the owner of discount variety stores. In October, Aritzia reported a 32% increase in year-over-year quarterly sales, benefiting from rapid physical expansion and strong customer demand for its "everyday luxury" pieces. As for Dollar Tree, the firm reported a strong year-over-year increase in sales and raised its full-year EPS guidance, citing demand from a broad spectrum of shoppers across income levels. We increased our positions in Aritzia and Dollar Tree this quarter, and both stocks ended the year among the fund's top-20 holdings.

Conversely, it hurt most to overweight Floor & Decor Holding (FND) (-17%) and Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS) (-12%). In October, Floor & Decor reported better-than-expected Q3 earnings but also experienced a 1.2% decline in same-store sales and issued a cautious outlook. This, combined with the challenging U.S. housing market and reduced appetite for large home renovation projects, weighed on the stock price. Meanwhile, shares of Dick's Sporting Goods were hampered by disappointing Q3 financial results and by concerns over the restructuring costs associated with the company's September acquisition of Foot Locker. Uncertainty over consumer spending habits going into the holiday season also hurt the stock. We added to the both positions this quarter on price weakness. Dick's was the fund's No. 12 holding at year end.

Largest Detractors Vs. Benchmark

Expedia Group Inc Class A (EXPE) Hotels, Resorts & Cruise Lines -0.51% -14
PVH Corp (PVH) Apparel, Accessories & Luxury Goods 0.51% -10

* 1 basis point = 0.01%.

Outlook and Positioning

In our view, there are numerous factors warranting a cautious near-term outlook for consumer discretionary stocks, especially amid tariff uncertainty. The U.S. demonstrated a mix of business cycle dynamics this quarter, with solid mid-cycle activity amid some areas of continued weakness in housing and labor markets. In addition, ongoing inflationary pressure – in conjunction with relatively high borrowing costs that significantly impact housing and durables – bears watching.

After two years of near-zero goods inflation, tariff hikes led to a rise in goods prices in the second half of 2025. Inflation remained sticky in housing and services, keeping headline consumer inflation elevated and notably higher than the Fed's 2% target.

But despite the challenges, the overall economy has remained quite resilient. The Fed cut its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25% percentage points in September, October and December, amid signs of softer employment conditions. The market and Fed expect two more rate cuts in 2026, due to expectations for soft labor markets and short-lived inflation.

Also, consumer savings and net worth continue to be robust for higher-income cohorts, who as a group are benefiting from near record-high financial market levels and home prices. Some categories within the consumer backdrop have held up much better than others. For example, services and experiences, such as travel and entertainment, have largely seen steady demand growth despite some volatility, whereas discretionary goods have been weaker. That said, a number of bigger-ticket discretionary areas are exhibiting signs of a modest rebound. Many companies, especially retailers, also are showing a surprising ability to quickly shift supply sourcing around from imported goods to domestic alternatives.

As always, while we believe it is important to be cognizant of the macro environment and broader sector backdrop, our investment process is primarily based on fundamental research and an evaluation of the specific prospects for individual securities. Many of the fund's top overweight positions are attractively valued stocks and have favorable risk/reward profiles, in our view, while simultaneously benefiting from defensive characteristics. As of year-end, we believe the fund is well-positioned for this uncertain environment, with its top-five relative overweights – Lowe's, Somnigroup, Amazon.com, Hilton Worldwide Holdings and Dick's Sporting Goods – all reflective of this description.

This quarter, we increased our holdings among several retailers where we are optimistic about longer-term growth prospects, including off-price retailers Ross Stores (ROST) and TJX (TJX); e-commerce giant Amazon.com (AMZN); and luxury brand retailers Tapestry (TPR) and Capri Holdings (CPRI). We also added to our exposure to home-related names in the home improvement retail, homebuilding and furnishing groups.

Conversely, we notably exited our positions in casinos & gaming names Penn Entertainment (PENN) and Caesars Entertainment (CZR).
