data.title: Monster Beverages: Focusing On Long-Term Drivers, Ignoring Short-Term Pressure
data.description:
Last spring, I assigned a strong buy rating to Monster Beverages (MNST), and the stock has since outperformed the market despite a significant decline this month. Plus 19% since publication is a decent result, but the dramatic reaction to the latest earnings report led to a 15% drop in shares, making it a good time to reassess my position and take action.

Despite the generally good report and strong numbers, the stock has come under pressure. I rechecked the TTM quantitative metrics and confirmed that the company's finances have not deteriorated. The management remains confident in its current position and future growth. Therefore, I maintain my strong buy rating, betting on long-term attractiveness and growth against short-term pressures.

High-Quality Idea

My approach to investing is systematic and algorithmic. I use my own 8-factor model to evaluate any business and assess its attractiveness. Last time, MNST scored 85/100. Today, it's 80/100. And this score is still associated with a strong buy rating or "attractive" status. The only variable in my approach that didn't exceed the desired threshold, separating MNST from the maximum rating, is future EPS growth compared to the current one. Expected EPS for Dec 2028 is $2.88, which implies a 3-year CAGR of approximately 13%. Actual EPS growth over the same period is 20%.

I see no negative aspects to all other valuation parameters. MNST continues to dominate the market, demonstrating high margins and management efficiency, while remaining virtually debt-free.

Fair Priced Business

Due to the quality of its business and its efficient model, MSNT has never been cheap in absolute terms, and the current non-GAAP valuation of 37x, even after a significant correction, confirms this. However, in my opinion, the current price offers a good opportunity to enter the stock for those who haven't yet done so, or to increase the position for loyal long-term investors.

My opinion is based on the historical multiplier method.In my previous review, the strong buy rating was set at roughly the same multiple level. Because then, and now, 37.2 (to be precise) is the median business valuation based on the 12 recent quarterly reports. Earnings per share growth over this time has led to a fair value by the historical multiples method of $74.4.

Given the company's strong financial rating, I consider these purchases at a fair value level more than justified.

As for the physical upside, the median to the top third of valuations over the same period is 14%, while the average Street target price of $87.46 suggests a potential approx 20%.

An outstanding company at an outstanding price.

Concerns About Falling Margins As The Main Risk

However, despite my positive outlook, the company's shares have been falling over the past month, and more sharply than the broader market. Clearly, the answer lies in analyzing the latest report, so I've carefully examined both the numbers and the earnings call.

My analysis and quantitative rating above take financial metrics into account, but let's briefly recap the key points.

The 2025 Q4 report exceeded analyst expectations and delivered record revenue and net profit TTM. Sales in the US grew 12.9% YoY, while the APAC region delivered the best growth at 16.8%. However, the market ignored this positive momentum.

The company was able to raise product prices without affecting sales, and innovation contributed significantly to its strong performance. Innovation in MNST's business means to me new product lines with strong marketing gimmicks. Currently, the flagship innovation is the Ultra line. As someone who gave up sugar many years ago, I can say that zero-sugar products are a powerful move. New products, their strong performance, and a robust pipeline—all of which the market has ignored.

Gross profit increased from 55.3% to 55.5% compared to Q4 2024. Operating efficiency also improved. Thanks to several factors, including a lower effective tax rate, the company was able to achieve significant growth in YoY net margin. The TTM net profit margin is 23%. This is a significant level for a consumer business, demonstrating the effectiveness of the business model and the presence of strong advantages, particularly effective marketing. But the market also took this for granted.

However, the market took notice of these words from Hilton Schlosberg,CEO & Vice Chairman:

Despite the modest impact on our business in the fourth quarter, the tariff landscape continues to be complicated and dynamic. For instance, tariffs significantly impacted the Midwest premium for aluminum, which increased the cost of our aluminum cans. We also import some raw materials into the United States, export certain raw materials for local markets and export limited quantities of finished goods.

A search of the document in my browser for the word "cost" yielded six hits, and as I understand it, the market has seized on this negative news. The projected increase in expenses and the increasing impact of tariffs could negatively impact the business's margins. Despite management providing no guidance in this context, the market used this topic to dampen the share price. Despite management clearly implying that dollar margins are significantly more important to the company than interest margins, the market decided to punish shareholders by reducing the company's market cap. But is this concern worth almost $14 billion (roughly how much Monster's price dropped after the report)?

As I interpreted the earnings call, the impact of the negative factors was offset by price increases. And future increases could continue. I don't know why the market didn't like this. I think it speaks to the solid position of the business. The company's margin reserve is high, and even if a negative cost-growth scenario materializes, the reserve will allow the company to demonstrate solid results and growth based on its marketing strategy and pipeline.

Bottom Line

In my opinion, I see a situation where short-term pressures are competing with long-term growth drivers. Forward margin compression has so frightened the market that pipelines, innovations, sustainable growth, and advantages like using Coca-Cola's distribution network have suddenly faded into the background. Well, in my view, this is an opportunity for long-term investors to add a quality asset at a fair price to their portfolios and bet on long-term fundamentals. MNSt remains one of my portfolio favorites.

data.title: Global Markets Swing On U.S.-Iran War Headlines As Risk-On Rally Falters - A Cross Analysis On S&P 500, U.S. Dollar Index, AUD/USD, And WTI Crude
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The global markets have once again seen a “wild swing of the pendulum” in the past eight hours, driven by conflicting news narratives related to the ongoing US-Iran war, which has lasted 25 days as of Tuesday, March 24, 2026.

Tuesday’s session marked a mild reversal from Monday’s sudden burst of risk-on behaviour triggered by US President Trump’s claim that the US and Iran are back on the negotiation table and a deal is imminent to end the conflict. Iranian officials refuted such claims, which led to a sell-off in global equities, a rebound in the US dollar, and a decline in benchmark oil prices, accompanied by an intraday softening of gold and silver.

By the end of Tuesday’s US session, three major benchmark US stock indices ended with losses: S&P 500 (-0.37%), Nasdaq 100 (-0.77%), and Dow Jones Industrial Average (-0.18%), with a slight risk-off backdrop backed by an increased odds that the US White House is sending military ground forces to Iran for a US-Israel joint offensive as media reports stated that the US administration has ordered the 82nd Airborne Division to deploy around 2,000 soldiers to the Middle East.

At around 4.15 am Singapore time, Wednesday, March 25 (right after the close of Tuesday’s US session), US President Trump gave a press conference that signalled that Iran had offered a “present” as a show of good faith in negotiations he has claimed are ongoing, hinting at a possible ceasefire to happen “very soon”.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures rose sharply at the start of the Wednesday, March 25, Asian session, with an intraday gain of 1% before it dwindled to 0.7% at this time of writing.

However, Polymarket’s prediction market platform is not reflecting an imminent US-Iran ceasefire, and several key cross assets’ price action behaviour is not displaying clear signals of an all-out risk-on herd sentiment at this juncture.

Unfolding them as follows...

Low odds of a ceasefire by March 31

Data sourced from Polymarket compiled by fundamental data provider MacroMicro has indicated that the prediction market-implied probability for a US-Iran ceasefire by March 31, 2026 has only increased slightly to 17.5% on Wednesday, March 25 at this time of writing from its prior day of 12.5% (see Fig. 1).

A higher probability of a ceasefire at 62.5% is being priced in at a longer period, by May 31, 2026 (a jump from 56.5% printed on Tuesday, March 24).

S&P 500 bulls get rejected again at the 200-day moving average

The current price actions of the US SPX 500 CFD index (a proxy of the S&P 500 E-mini futures) have its intraday rally cut short again at its 200-day moving average, now coinciding with the 6,648 key short-term pivotal resistance (see Fig. 2).

A breakdown with an hourly close below 6,540 near-term support (downside trigger) may open up scope for the bears to retest Monday’s March 23, 2026 swing low area at 6,470/442 in the first step.

The US Dollar Index remains supported by its 20-day moving average

The recent 1.6% decline seen in the US Dollar Index from its 100.10/100.54 medium-term range support has managed and continued to find support at the 99.00 level, which confluences with the 20-day moving average and the ascending trend line in place since the January 27, 2026 low of 95.55 (see Fig. 3).

AUD/USD bulls capped below the 50-day moving average

The Australian dollar is considered a proxy of risk appetite, where a rally in the AUD/USD represents risk-on behaviour.

On Wednesday, March 25, the Asian opening session intraday rally seen in the AUD/USD has been wiped out after a bearish reaction right at the 50-day moving average, with an intraday loss of 0.4% at this time of writing.

Watch the 0.7027 key short-term pivotal resistance on the AUD/USD, and it has staged a bearish breakdown from a minor “Head & Shoulders” bearish reversal configuration on Monday, March 23, 2026 (see Fig. 4).

A break below 0.6910 key medium-term pivotal support exposes further potential weakness towards the next intermediate support at 0.6838 in the first step.

WTI crude oil is still holding above the 20-day moving average

Wednesday’s Asian opening session sell-off seen in the West Texas Oil CFD index (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) has managed to find support once again at the 20-day moving average, which also stalled Monday’s intraday plunge of 10% triggered by US President Trump’s claim of a new round of negotiations between the US and Iran.

Watch the $88.36/85.50 key short-term pivotal support, and a clearance with an hourly close above $93.70 may see a further push up to retest the minor range resistance of $102.25 (see Fig. 5).

Conclusion

An analysis of the current price structures of the related cross-asset classes, S&P 500, US Dollar Index, AUD/USD, and West Texas crude oil suggests that we are not out of the woods yet for a significant bullish reversal in risk appetite to take form.

Perhaps the market is rebuking US President Trump’s claims of an imminent ceasefire between the US, Israel, and Iran.

data.title: PDD Q4 Earnings Miss: I Warned You About The End Of Their Hypergrowth
data.description:
Introduction

Back in November, I wrote an article and warned that the era of hyper-growth for PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) was coming to an end, and unfortunately for the shareholders, the market thereafter decided to agree with me, sending the stock down. At the time, I was skeptical regarding Temu’s sustainability, noting how its aggressive tactics reminded me far too much of the early days of Wish, which did not end well, even though both the results and the stock were surging at some point in 2020. Besides, I said that even a cheap valuation multiple couldn't protect investors from the massive geopolitical risks that weigh on all of the Chinese stocks.

Since that analysis, the stock is down by about 20%, as both of my arguments proved to be correct. PDD stock is down together with all of its Chinese peers, and the recent Q4 earnings show the company isn’t doing as well as everyone hoped it would.

PDD missed on both revenue and earnings expectations, with quarterly revenue of RMB123.91 billion and an adjusted EPS of RMB17.69 coming in below what analysts expected to see. While the company did manage a 12% revenue increase for the quarter, the broader trend doesn’t look great, and management decided that it was time to make heavy supply chain investments that they admit will "inevitably affect" financial performance. In this article, I’ll break down this quarter and try to answer the question of whether it is a compelling buying opportunity now, after the stock went down by 20%.

“The external environment and competitive landscape are undergoing rapid changes. To meet the evolving needs of consumers, we must continually explore and make investments,” said Ms. Jun Liu, VP of Finance of PDD Holdings. “These investments are firm and long-term, and will inevitably affect our financial performance.” - Earnings Release.

Q4 2025

As I already mentioned, total revenues were of RMB123,912.2 million, which was a 12% increase year-over-year. While 12% it’s not a weak growth, and actually, it was better than any other quarter of 2025, the company has still missed the target of RMB124.71 billion that analysts predicted they’d see. Anyway, the days of explosive growth with 60% to 120% growth year-over-year are long gone, and the company isn’t coming back there.

There are two areas from which the money comes - online marketing services, which brought in RMB60,010.1 million, and transaction services, with RMB63,902.1 million, which was an increase of 19%. Once again, even though the numbers are not straightforwardly low, it is very far away from how the company was going before, throughout 2024.

Net income did not do as well as revenue (and yes, even though the revenue had disappointed). It came in at RMB24,541.0 million, which is an 11% drop from the last quarter of 2024, which is a concerning sign. Non-GAAP diluted earnings per ADS also did not reach the estimate of RMB20.71. It means business operations cost more, but the revenues are not rising by the same measure. The reasons are various, starting from the competition to the lower consumer spending, and it weighs on margins. By the way, not only is PDD struggling, but retail is weak for its competitors as well.

Breaking down what happened exactly, we would see that the total costs of revenues increased 15% to RMB55,155.6 million, largely driven by higher fulfillment fees and server costs. This is a major confirmation of my thesis regarding Temu, as fulfillment fees are the logistics of getting a package delivered to the customer. PDD takes these costs on itself to keep people ordering, and its heavy expansion into the international markets contributes to this massive burden.

Once again, it’s like Wish all over again. I remember those days so well, as I had a YouTube channel then, where a host talked mostly about Wish breaking down the business model and the stock price. There was a market frenzy over it, as during the pandemic, all of the physical retailers were shutting down, and digital shopping was everything. The stock was unstoppable until the time came to monetize those acquired users with shipping and higher prices. The moment that happened, everyone returned to order from Aliexpress. On the digital side, bandwidth and server costs also add up as apps and websites run for millions of simultaneous users globally.

Operating expenses are up 10%, and sales and marketing are a huge expense for obvious reasons. The competition is enormous, so this is a necessary investment to keep selling. What was indeed unexpected was the 32% surge in research and development spending, meaning the company tries to find other ways besides sales to attract new people, but it doesn’t seem to be translating into revenue, at least for now.

At the same time, for the full year of 2025, the situation isn’t horrible. The company performed, even if relatively slowly, with RMB431,845.7 million in total revenue, which was a 10% increase from 2024. Full-year operating profit decreased by 13% to RMB94,624.1 million. The company is having to spend significantly more just to maintain its current position, without even reaccelerating in growth. Net cash generated from operating activities went down to RMB106,938.7 million compared to over RMB121 billion the year before. As I already mentioned, it looks like the situation will only worsen, and the management acknowledges it, making their biggest bet on supply chain investment, which is a huge shift for this company, as in the past, PDD used to be a company with a capital-light business model, connecting buyers and sellers. By investing in the supply chain, they are beginning to build warehouses, logistics, and everything else needed to survive the changing competitive landscape.

Whether it will be a successful shift, only time will tell, but it is definitely a very risky decision. Alibaba’s (BABA) infrastructure isn’t helping its retail segment to grow, so I’m not sure how PDD will be different.

Despite the miss and the risky future, PDD has a lot of cash, and it is a huge plus in these uncertain times. As of December 31, 2025, their cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totaled RMB422.3 billion, up from RMB331.6 billion a year earlier. This gives them a huge safety net, but for me, it raises the question of whether this business model is not too expensive to keep.

Valuation

Looking at the valuation, PDD is cheap, but there is a reason for this. It is way cheaper than the sector, and even more at a discount compared to its own 5-year average, but the growth has slowed significantly as well.

Even though the company is still incredibly profitable and has a huge pile of cash (RMB422.3 billion to be exact, which is about $60 billion), the risk profile has changed. Management itself is warning that the high-margin days are over, and now the supply chain will be a heavy investment they have to make.

Conclusion

To conclude, it is clear that PDD is still not in a bad position; it is not shrinking, but the results I am seeing today are another confirmation that the easy growth is gone, and it is not coming back. The management is being very transparent about the fact that building out the supply chain will drag on profits for the foreseeable future. There are no promises when we will see results from this investment. For me, the risks I saw back in November (from the Temu and the geopolitical environment) are still here, while another risk of pivoting away from an asset-light business model adds additional uncertainty.

Interestingly, the stock is actually up as I am writing this, surging about 2% in pre-market trading despite the earnings miss. It is most likely due to the low valuation multiple, mixed with hopes for an agreement between the U.S. and Iran, and also the company’s massive RMB422.3 billion cash position, which smooths the worries of the market. However, I am not a fan of this specific setup, and also not a fan of investing in Chinese companies while geopolitical tensions are so pronounced. I am rating PDD a Hold, but I’d not consider a position myself for now.
